title: Zambia should commit to tackling toxic lead mine's legacy – The Mail & Guardian
impact_evidence: Environmental | Air & Climate | Dust generation -> "Lead dust from these waste dumps, which are not covered, continues to blow over to nearby residential areas and threaten community health." || Environmental | Air & Climate | Air quality degradation (dust, diesel exhaust, blasting emissions) -> "Lead dust from these waste dumps, which are not covered, continues to blow over to nearby residential areas and threaten community health." || Environmental | Land, Soil & Geology | Tailings generation and management -> "The former mine area contains more than three-million tonnes of waste from the mining process and about 2.5-million tonnes of waste from the smelter." | "the toxic waste that remained was never cleaned up." || Social | Health, Safety & Well-being | Community exposure to dust, noise, pollution -> "Residents in Kabwe, Central Zambia, have been exposed to highly toxic lead for decades." | "An estimated 200 000 Kabwe residents are exposed to lead-polluted homes backyards, schools, play areas and roads." || Social | Health, Safety & Well-being | Mental and physical health impacts on communities -> "A 2018 medical study estimated that more than 95% of children in townships exposed to lead from the Kabwe mine have elevated blood-lead levels, and about half the children in the townships have such high blood-lead levels that they urgently require medical intervention." | "It can cause hearing loss, vision loss, high blood pressure, IQ deficits, behavioural problems and even coma, convulsions, and death.
